the gap statement ('no prior work has disentangled...') is asserted without support; improving the draft plausibly requires reconstructing the thesis arc and sharpening the framing of what is actually being claimed.","R1.2":"The argument moves from failure-mode identification → two-source decomposition → system design → results without fully closing the loop: the paper claims the gating layer is the key differentiator but the logical connection between the two entropy scores and the learned gate is never made explicit; consistency across that chain is centrally expected in any improvement task.","R1.3":"Results are stated (AUROC 0.78, 64% usefulness rating) without anchoring them to the evaluation protocol described; the 5000-question subset and the three additional annotations are mentioned but their relationship to the reported numbers is underspecified, making evidence-claim alignment a live concern.","R1.5":"Implementation details appear as a free-standing third paragraph after baselines are introduced, fragmenting the narrative; the contribution statement belongs in an introduction-style position rather than embedded in an architecture description; structural reordering is a natural and expected component of draft improvement.","R1.6":"Multiple sentences are severely overloaded (the contribution sentence runs ~65 words across two dashes); writing polish is load-bearing whenever the delegation is the open-ended 'improve this draft.'"},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should I prioritize writing-level improvements (sentence clarity, concision, flow) or argument-level improvements (contribution framing, logical structure, evidence grounding)?"}

rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim is buried inside a 60-word sentence mid-second-paragraph; the gap statement ('no prior work has disentangled...') is asserted without support; improving the draft plausibly requires reconstructing the thesis arc and sharpening the framing of what is actually being claimed.
rationale.R1.2: The argument moves from failure-mode identification → two-source decomposition → system design → results without fully closing the loop: the paper claims the gating layer is the key differentiator but the logical connection between the two entropy scores and the learned gate is never made explicit; consistency across that chain is centrally expected in any improvement task.
rationale.R1.3: Results are stated (AUROC 0.78, 64% usefulness rating) without anchoring them to the evaluation protocol described; the 5000-question subset and the three additional annotations are mentioned but their relationship to the reported numbers is underspecified, making evidence-claim alignment a live concern.
rationale.R1.5: Implementation details appear as a free-standing third paragraph after baselines are introduced, fragmenting the narrative; the contribution statement belongs in an introduction-style position rather than embedded in an architecture description; structural reordering is a natural and expected component of draft improvement.
rationale.R1.6: Multiple sentences are severely overloaded (the contribution sentence runs ~65 words across two dashes); writing polish is load-bearing whenever the delegation is the open-ended 'improve this draft.'

parsed.clarification_question: Should I prioritize writing-level improvements (sentence clarity, concision, flow) or argument-level improvements (contribution framing, logical structure, evidence grounding)?
